startScreenOptions.title: BI8.1-BI8.6 — Interactive Lecture (BI)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 4 embedded questions for BI8.1-BI8.6.

interactions.label: A patient with obstructive jaundice is at risk of deficiency of vitamins A, D, E

params.question: A patient with obstructive jaundice is at risk of deficiency of vitamins A, D, E, and K. Which step in the fat-soluble vitamin absorption pathway is most directly impaired in this patient?

answers.text: Conversion of provitamins to active forms inside the intestinal mucosa

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Provitamin conversion (e.g., beta-carotene to retinal) occurs inside enterocytes, after the vitamins have already been absorbed. The block in obstructive jaundice occurs earlier — in the gut lumen, where bile is needed to form micelles.

answers.text: Formation of micelles by bile salts in the duodenum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Bile salts emulsify dietary fats and form micelles that carry fat-soluble vitamins to the enterocyte surface. Without bile reaching the duodenum, this critical step fails and the vitamins pass through unabsorbed.

Chylomicron packaging occurs inside enterocytes after the vitamins have already crossed the gut wall. The defect in obstructive jaundice is upstream — bile cannot reach the lumen to form micelles in the first place.

answers.text: Transport of chylomicrons through the lymphatic system

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Lymphatic transport occurs after absorption and chylomicron assembly are complete. The problem in obstructive jaundice is at the earliest luminal step — micelle formation, which requires bile salts.

overallFeedback.feedback: The correct answer is micelle formation by bile salts. In obstructive jaundice, bile cannot reach the duodenum. Without bile salts, dietary fats and fat-soluble vitamins cannot form micelles and remain unabsorbed in the gut lumen.

Exactly right. Bile salts are essential for emulsifying fats and forming micelles that deliver fat-soluble vitamins to enterocytes. Any condition blocking bile flow — obstructive jaundice, biliary atresia — leads to malabsorption of all four fat-soluble vitamins.

interactions.label: Vitamin D deficiency is rare in India because the country receives abundant sunl

params.question: Vitamin D deficiency is rare in India because the country receives abundant sunlight throughout the year.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This statement is actually false. Despite India being a tropical country, 70 to 90 percent of its population is vitamin D deficient. Several factors — limited sun exposure, full-body clothing, higher skin melanin reducing UV absorption, and phytate-rich diets — collectively impair vitamin D synthesis even in a sunny climate.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this is false. Despite abundant sunlight, 70 to 90 percent of the Indian population is vitamin D deficient. Limited outdoor exposure, full-body clothing, high melanin content in the skin (which reduces ultraviolet B absorption), and phytate-rich diets all impair vitamin D synthesis and absorption.

interactions.label: Which pair of B-complex vitamins both support DNA synthesis, and whose deficienc

params.question: Which pair of B-complex vitamins both support DNA synthesis, and whose deficiency produces megaloblastic anaemia?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Thiamine forms the coenzyme for pyruvate dehydrogenase (energy metabolism), and riboflavin powers the electron transport chain. Neither is a cofactor for DNA synthesis, and their deficiencies cause beriberi and angular stomatitis respectively.

Correct. Folate supplies single-carbon units for nucleotide synthesis, and cobalamin supports methionine regeneration. Both are essential for DNA production; when either is deficient, red cell precursors cannot divide properly, producing large immature megaloblasts.

Pyridoxine is the coenzyme for amino acid transamination, and biotin acts as a carboxylase cofactor. Their deficiencies produce peripheral neuropathy and dermatitis respectively — not megaloblastic anaemia.

Pantothenic acid builds coenzyme A for the Krebs cycle, and niacin forms NAD/NADP for redox reactions. Both are energy metabolism coenzymes, not DNA synthesis cofactors. Niacin deficiency causes pellagra, not megaloblastic anaemia.

overallFeedback.feedback: The correct pair is folate (B9) and cobalamin (B12). Folate provides single-carbon units for nucleotide synthesis, while B12 supports methionine regeneration. Both are required for DNA production, and deficiency of either produces megaloblastic anaemia with large immature red cell precursors.

Well done. Folate (B9) and cobalamin (B12) are the paired vitamins of DNA synthesis. Their deficiency causes megaloblastic anaemia, though B12 deficiency additionally causes subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord — a key differentiating feature.

interactions.label: During a community nutrition survey, a 3-year-old child has normal weight-for-he

params.question: During a community nutrition survey, a 3-year-old child has normal weight-for-height but significantly low height-for-age on WHO Z-scores. This anthropometric pattern indicates:

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Wasting is defined by low weight-for-height, which reflects acute or recent undernutrition. This child has normal weight-for-height, so wasting is not the correct classification.

Correct. Low height-for-age with normal weight-for-height is the hallmark of stunting — the child has adapted to prolonged inadequate nutrition by reducing linear growth. Stunting reflects chronic undernutrition accumulated over months or years.

Severe acute malnutrition is identified by very low weight-for-height, mid-upper arm circumference below 11.5 cm, or nutritional oedema. This child's weight-for-height is normal, so severe acute malnutrition is not indicated.

answers.text: Normal nutritional status with constitutional short stature

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While constitutional short stature exists, low height-for-age on WHO Z-scores in a community nutrition survey is classified as stunting (chronic undernutrition) unless a clinical evaluation rules it out. In the ABCD framework, anthropometric findings guide the initial assessment.

overallFeedback.feedback: The correct answer is chronic undernutrition (stunting). In the 'A' (anthropometric) component of the ABCD framework, low height-for-age indicates stunting from chronic undernutrition, while low weight-for-height indicates wasting from acute undernutrition. This child's pattern — low height-for-age with normal weight-for-height — is classic stunting.

Correct. Stunting (low height-for-age) reflects chronic undernutrition — the body has reduced linear growth over a prolonged period. Around half of Indian children under five show stunting or wasting, illustrating the country's double burden of malnutrition.
